video_title: Dan Ives: Palantir Could Be a $1 TRILLION Company
gist: Wedbush's Dan Ives argues the AI buildout's biggest risk is not valuation but a self-inflicted tech 'PR problem' (job-loss messaging plus rising electricity bills threatening data-center town approvals), while staying bullish on Apple's Gemini-powered AI 'toll collector' model, Microsoft/Meta/Salesforce as lagging install-base monetization stories, and Palantir as a future trillion-dollar company. The pod closes with Ives, Dan Nathan and Danny Moses dissecting the SpaceX IPO as a Musk-bet 'derivative AI play' priced via exceptional index rule accommodations.

themes.theme: AI's PR Problem as the Real Macro Risk

insights.claim: Dan Ives argues the biggest risk to the AI trade is not valuation or capex but whether the use cases actually work and whether the spending nets out, framing 'do the use cases actually work' as the central question.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Reframes the bear case away from multiples toward ROI on spend, which is the variable investors should track rather than valuation alone.
insights.quote: The biggest risk for everything see I don't mean risk is valuation capbacks do the use cases actually work is the spending net the biggest risk in the whole thing

insights.claim: Tech executives' messaging on AI-driven job losses (Anthropic's Amodei, Microsoft's Mustafa Suleyman saying AI will cut 50% of lawyers) has pushed tech approval below TSA lines in US surveys, creating a self-inflicted political liability.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Negative public sentiment toward tech becomes a regulatory and approval headwind, not just a narrative problem.
insights.quote: When you do that, you're below TSA lines in terms of surveys in the United States

insights.claim: Data centers must be voted in by towns and cities, so the combination of AI job-loss fears and rising electricity bills directly threatens local approval of the buildout that the entire AI trade depends on.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Local-permitting risk is an underpriced bottleneck; watch town/city approval rates and utility-rate politics as leading indicators for data-center supply.
insights.quote: If I tell the average American AI is going to take away your job and it's going to increase your electricity bill

insights.claim: Sam Altman has shifted his tone away from emphasizing AI job displacement (visible in his Faber/CNBC interview), which Ives reads as a PR pivot but Nathan reads as Altman opportunistically saying whatever is useful at the time.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives and Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Signals the AI labs are now managing political optics; reduced confidence in Altman's statements is a credibility discount to watch.
insights.quote: If you notice, no longer he's like AI job because it is true

insights.claim: Ives characterizes data centers as 'the hearts and lungs of the AI revolution' that must be approved and built out, making approval/buildout the single biggest near-term focus for the sector.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Positions data-center approval cadence as the core gating factor for AI revenue realization.
insights.quote: data centers are the hearts and lungs of the AI revol you need them approved built out

themes.theme: Apple's AI Foundation and the Toll-Collector Thesis

insights.claim: Apple at WWDC finally delivered a usable AI foundation for developers built with Google's Gemini as the core partner, ending two years of unfulfilled Apple Intelligence promises where roughly 98% of the 2024 pitch never shipped.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: A real developer platform is the prerequisite for Apple's AI monetization; the soft stock reaction may be a buying setup if developer adoption follows.
insights.quote: they finally admitted they're never gonna be anthropic or open AI

insights.claim: Ives frames Apple as the 'toll collector on the highway' for consumers heading down the AI path, leveraging 1.5 billion iPhones (400 million not upgraded in two years) rather than trying to build a frontier model.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Apple's edge is distribution/monetization of AI through its install base, not model leadership; an 'adequate' AI is sufficient for the bull case.
insights.quote: They're essentially going to be the toll collector on the highway for consumers as they head down the AI path

insights.claim: Ives sees a roughly $400 Apple price target driven by services GMs potentially exceeding 50%, services growth reaccelerating to mid-teens, and ~300 million users upgrading to an AI-driven device into the 20th-anniversary year.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Lays out the explicit margin/mix-shift path from ~$290 to ~$400; track services GM and growth reacceleration as the proof points.

insights.claim: China has flipped from a multi-quarter decline to a ~20% growth tailwind for Apple, but Apple Intelligence won't be available in China and will require a local partner like Alibaba plus regulatory work.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: China reacceleration supports the bull case but AI rollout there is gated on local-cloud partnerships and regulators.

insights.claim: Nathan is skeptical that on-device AI is necessary given that Claude and Gemini apps already act as capable phone assistants accessing Gmail and contacts, pushing back on Apple's security-driven on-device rationale.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: If third-party AI apps already deliver assistant functionality, Apple's on-device moat thesis is weaker than the bull case assumes.

insights.claim: Microsoft is being mispriced as 'disrupted by AI' (down ~30% from mid-2025 highs) when its enterprise Azure base is only ~10% monetized; Ives targets $550-$600 as Copilot fixes and deal acceleration hit a 'sweet spot.'
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Frames Microsoft weakness as a monetization-timing problem, not structural disruption; the $550-$600 target is the upside if execution lands.

insights.claim: Microsoft's Azure capacity shortfall has cost roughly 400-500 basis points of Azure growth, and OpenAI is diversifying away from Azure while OpenAI tech has underperformed inside Copilot, prompting Microsoft to invest in Anthropic.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Quantifies the capacity drag on Azure and signals Microsoft hedging its model dependency away from OpenAI.
insights.quote: it's probably taken off four or 500 bips of growth off Azure

insights.claim: Nathan questions why memory/storage suppliers like Micron didn't add capacity a year ago given Nvidia being sold out for years, implying skepticism about whether hyperscalers' multi-year contract visibility is now credible.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Raises a credibility question on multi-year demand forecasts; if visibility was missed before, current 3-5 year contract assumptions may be overconfident.

insights.claim: Microsoft's Copilot has only reached single-digit-percentage penetration of its ~400 million Office 365 seats (roughly 15-20 million seats), which Ives frames as untapped upside once the product is fixed.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan and Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Low Copilot attach rate is either a large runway (bull) or evidence of weak product-market fit (bear); seat growth is the key metric.

insights.claim: Meta (down ~25% from late-2025 highs) is in a 'show me' period like Microsoft and Oracle, with monetization of its capex and Meta AI across 3.5 billion users not expected to show in advertising until roughly Q4.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Sets Q4 as the inflection window for Meta AI ad monetization; the Instagram subscription is only a small early step.

insights.claim: Salesforce is stuck near 52-week lows because Agentforce, despite crossing $1 billion, is only ~3-4% of revenue and won't move the needle until it reaches 10-15%, making it a slow-burn rather than a near-term reacceleration.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Agentforce penetration must roughly triple-to-quadruple as a revenue share before Salesforce re-rates; until then it underperforms peers.

insights.claim: Ives argues large install bases (Apple, Microsoft, Oracle, and Google a year ago) let a company mess up one product cycle without losing customers, because switching costs and IT-department lock-in retain the base.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: A reusable mental model for buying temporarily-stumbling incumbents: install-base lock-in buys time to fix the product.
insights.quote: when you have that install base, you can mess up one piece because that install base is not going to leave if you're able to ultimately figure it out

insights.claim: Google's Gemini-integrated productivity suite is unlikely to displace Microsoft Office in the Global 2000 because of standardization, compliance, switching costs and IT departments built around Microsoft, even as startups default to Google.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Enterprise lock-in caps Google's productivity threat to Microsoft at the high end despite small-business adoption.

themes.theme: Palantir as a Future Trillion-Dollar Name

insights.claim: Ives believes Palantir will eventually reach a trillion-dollar valuation because as enterprise and government adoption ramps, Palantir becomes the 'lifeline' intermediary in a world where LLMs commoditize.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: The long thesis rests on LLM commoditization elevating data/orchestration intermediaries; Palantir is the preferred expression.
insights.quote: my view is that over time Palunteer will be a trillion dollar valuation

insights.claim: Palantir's stock weakness (flat year-over-year, down ~25% from highs, ~$315B market cap) is less about Palantir itself and more about the broader de-rating of data names like Datadog, Snowflake and MongoDB.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Attributes Palantir's drawdown to sector sentiment rather than fundamentals, supporting a buy-the-dip stance.
insights.quote: what's happened to Palunteer stock is less about Palunteer and it's more about data dog snowflake in data

insights.claim: Palantir raised FY guidance dramatically to ~$11 billion in sales (versus ~$7.5 billion expected at the start of the year), with ~90%+ earnings growth and ~75% sales growth this year, and ~mid-40s growth expected next year.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Concrete guidance beat quantifies the fundamental momentum underpinning the valuation; free cash flow will drive the ultimate multiple.

insights.claim: Roughly 90% of companies signing Palantir deals are new customers, which Ives cites as evidence Palantir is the only vendor that can deliver real enterprise AI use cases and is not threatened by an Anthropic 'SaaS apocalypse.'
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: High new-logo mix supports the unreplicable-core narrative; counters the bear thesis that LLM vendors disintermediate Palantir.
insights.quote: probably 90% of companies that we knew doing deals with Palunteer today are essentially new customers

insights.claim: Ives dismisses Anthropic as 'a wrapper' and calls Palantir's core technology 'unreplicable,' aligning with Karp's long-standing view that LLMs commoditize while data and orchestration retain value.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: A sharply differentiated take that the value accrues to the data/integration layer rather than the model layer.
insights.quote: Anthropic is a rapper. The core technology that Palunteer has is unreplicable

insights.claim: Ives predicts the LLM landscape fragments into hundreds of vertical models (e.g., construction, a Malaysian financial-company LLM), which is why the AI labs are pivoting hard toward enterprise.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Vertical-LLM proliferation favors enterprise-distribution and integration plays over a winner-take-all model market.
insights.quote: you'll have like hundreds of LLMs. There'll be verticals

insights.claim: Anthropic's ARR scaling from ~$10 billion to ~$40 billion (largely enterprise, without government) is unprecedented, leading Nathan to question why an investor wouldn't prefer Anthropic over Palantir even at a trillion-dollar valuation.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan and Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Frames the direct comparison between buying the model layer (hypergrowth, unprofitable) versus the integration layer (Palantir).

themes.theme: Semis, Software Volatility and Trading Behavior

insights.claim: Ives stays bullish on Nvidia and AMD as two core chip players plus the memory complex (SK Hynix, Micron, SanDisk), and reads weekend Taiwan supply checks showing tighter supply as positive 'tea leaves' for early next year.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Supply-chain checks pointing to tighter Asia supply support the chip-demand-durability call against the recent selloff.
insights.quote: Look at our Taiwan checks this weekend showed tighter supply over the last few weeks

insights.claim: Ives attributes last week's chip 'wreck' (SMH down 9% Friday, down 6% intraday Tuesday) primarily to the Broadcom-Google/TPU news rather than a sector-wide 'cockroach,' rejecting the read-through to broad semi weakness.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: If the selloff is idiosyncratic to Broadcom/Google rather than systemic, the broad semi dip is a buying opportunity.
insights.quote: I think it's more Google and t I mean, that's that's my view

insights.claim: Investors treat software names as near-term trading vehicles because they are 'not paid to own these through earnings' since the stocks reliably sell off post-print, favoring chips over SaaS.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Explains persistent software underperformance as a positioning/behavioral dynamic; IGV bounced from ~74 (April 10 low) to ~92.5.
insights.quote: investors are not paid to own these through earnings because no matter what happen, they sell off

insights.claim: Ives is constructive on Intel's turnaround under CEO 'Whip,' betting Intel becomes a viable third foundry option, while Nathan is skeptical given Intel missed mobile, servers and roughly five CEOs over 15 years.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives and Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Foundry optionality is the bull case for Intel; the market is granting a multiple on future foundry revenue that hinges on unproven execution.

insights.claim: Ives evokes a CEO-credibility pattern (Arvind Krishna at IBM, Nadella early at Microsoft, McDermott) where a leader 'gets it' and you don't bet against them, applying it to Intel's new CEO and AMD's Lisa Su.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: A management-quality heuristic for turnaround bets; CEO conviction is treated as a leading signal for re-rating.

insights.claim: Nathan warns of irrational exuberance in semis after the group ran ~100% in two months with widening volatility bands, arguing investors only need one fundamental reason to sell a crowded trade.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Flags fragile positioning in the market's leadership group; a single catalyst (energy bottlenecks, ROI doubts, populism) could trigger a sharp unwind.
insights.quote: if you didn't think that there was um irrational exuberance there, you're not paying attention

themes.theme: SpaceX IPO and the Musk Merger Bet

insights.claim: SpaceX is being treated as the market's 'first test' and a derivative AI play rather than a true AI or chip play, with investors essentially buying into Musk and a multi-year (2-4 year) vision spanning Starlink and data centers in space.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Frames SpaceX as a Musk-conviction/long-duration bet akin to playing tech in the early '90s, not a near-term fundamentals trade.
insights.quote: SpaceX is a derivative AI revolution

insights.claim: Ives assigns an over-80% probability that Tesla merges with SpaceX in 2027, which would give Musk control and let locked-up SpaceX holders hedge via Tesla, partly explaining Tesla's support.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: A specific, high-conviction merger call with cross-asset hedging implications; Tesla becomes a partial proxy for SpaceX exposure.
insights.quote: It's my view there's an over 80% chance that Tesla ultimately will merge with SpaceX in 2027

insights.claim: SpaceX received exceptional index accommodations: S&P kept its profitability bar (excluding SpaceX), but Footsie Russell allows entry after a few trading days and the NASDAQ-100 admits it at 3x float (~$225B), enabling fast indexer buying.
insights.speaker: Danny Moses and Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Rule exceptions create forced index demand and an unusual technical setup; Moses notes rules being made exceptional is rare in his career.

insights.claim: Danny Moses argues the small float relative to the ~$225B size plus large numbers of locked-up holders who cannot sell could cause SpaceX to double on day one, with the stock unlikely to trade on fundamentals out of the gate.
insights.speaker: Danny Moses
insights.so_what: Float-and-lockup mechanics, not valuation, may dominate initial price action; expect a technical pop rather than a fundamental print.

insights.claim: XAI loses over $1 billion a month yet now has ~$2.25 billion/month in compute revenue starting fall 2026 from Anthropic (~$1B) and Google (~$1B) renting its ~220,000 idle Nvidia GPUs, raising the question of why those GPUs sat unused.
insights.speaker: Dan Nathan
insights.so_what: Highlights an apparent contradiction in the AI-scarcity narrative (idle GPUs) while quantifying XAI's emerging compute-rental revenue from its own competitors.

insights.claim: Ives maintains Tesla price targets of ~$600 base case and ~$800 bull case based on autonomous, Optimus and physical AI, and would still favor Tesla over SpaceX while expecting an eventual merger.
insights.speaker: Dan Ives
insights.so_what: Provides explicit Tesla valuation anchors and frames Tesla as the preferred vehicle even ahead of the anticipated SpaceX merger.
